speaker: Rob Moore
words: The following tape was recorded on the 3rd of July, 1973, on the porch at the home of Mr. and Mrs. Marvin Long. The voices on the tape are those of Marvin Long, his wife Martha, and Rob Moore, the interviewer. The Longs live in the residential section of Kendrick, Idaho, in a house built by the original draftsman of the town plat for Kendrick, a man named Kirby. Background noise is the traffic on the street outside the Long home.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: And he had a number of business, what we call his useful business ventures that are really interesting considering he's a, well now you're junk business, you know that is.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Is that on though?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I think so. Let me see, yeah it's going. Well tell me about your junk business.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Me?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh. Right.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Let's see, when was that back in?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Let me get this picture.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: What year was I sick?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well anyway I was sick a year I'd been working store all morning. You were about 21. 21. And I was sick a whole year of nervous breakdown. And the first time I got up with my clothes on I weighed 101 pounds so you might know it. Right. And all I had was debts but when I was a kid I bought junk and sold it.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: And I started out and I had a, see did I have a horse?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I had a horse, one horse, my neighbor over here.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: He had one of these democrat wagons, you know what a democrat wagon is?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: It's one of those small, just a small wagon.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah the little one.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why was it called a democrat wagon?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I don't know. I don't know but that's the nickname for it. It's a small wagon. You know there was two types, there was the big ones they hauled rain in, you know, the forest team should go and then they had this little one for delight work, you know, run around. These little wagons. Anyway I went over there and least rented this wagon and I had a horse and I think I paid him a dollar and a quarter a day to rental instead of renting a car. Right. And I went out buying junk and I made this whole territory home country and I must have worth about $300 anyway when I got through.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of junk were you getting?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: At that time it was hides and horse hair from the farmers they saved. Well it occurred you didn't know how the tails were made. That was a good price but you didn't get them with the weight of it. Hides and brass, copper and rubber, the whole rubber chunk. I was a regular. Wool too. Yeah, wool, anything, you could get a market for it. And I cleaned up and paid all my expenses that summer. I owed $300.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where did you peddle the stuff?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I had a stated trip in a fur company, they're own business in Spokane. I had a method, the buyer come down every Saturday and bought it and shipped it to Spokane. I had a good market for all that stuff.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: How long did you stay out on these trips to the ridges?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I stayed out a week.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Friday.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you usually travel along one ridge on a trip?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, I worked them all. I worked everything along here. I didn't miss any places. I got a lot of free to meal, a lot of sleep, I didn't sleep in Wagon Island every night. They just brought me in. I stayed one place, stayed a whole week, one place, they wouldn't let me go anywhere else.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did you stay in that business?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, that summer, until I got out of debt.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Then you went back to work in the store again?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, I went back to the store.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, just to get out of debt, I couldn't know a dollar. Yeah, people were good to me though. And then he's outdoor, was recommended, you know, when he was well. Well, you know, most of you know I weighed 101 pounds.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, was the store a better business than the junk business? Well, that's when you went to school, isn't it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, I was just a kid. I was working for wages and stores. I never got into this. I went back into this. I came back here in 1920.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I was 26 years old when I went into the books and business. Of course, I stayed with them until I was the last one left. There were three of us brothers, my dad and I, when we originally started. Of course, we had, we did farm, had cattle, slaughterhouse, grocery store, and we had some rice goods and meats.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did the store business change over the years, you know, like as things changed, you know, as cars came in?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, as the cars came in, people come to town off there and bought smaller orders and competition becomes more and more competitive. They didn't drive to Lewiston or Moscow or wherever it is at, in horse and buggy days, but that's when the cars come in, you had to get more competitive.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: They didn't go like they do now, but they still went. Of course, it probably don't make any difference where you're at nowadays with the cars.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: The distance from Moscow to Lewiston to Kendrick is the same distance as from Kendrick to Lewiston, did you know that?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, there's little, what I'm getting at is a little business sense to that too. See, there's so much, two points for me, how far it is back and forth. Well, I had, I worked up quite, I knew quite a few people here that went to Lewiston and worked, everybody goes to Lewiston and works a job, you don't have to ask them where they're working because they went down and worked for the Potlatch Forest, because that's our payroll. Well, I got a bunch of new ones down there and they'd come up on Saturdays and kept increasing, and they'd come up on Saturdays and put their work put. Now Lewiston, that works both ways, if you're a car driver, you got a Fox -AMC, so it can be done, I found that out.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, when people started, people started traveling more and you had to compete more, did you kind of change, you had to change your way of doing business, change your line of goods?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, you changed your way of doing business, you had to get competitive, you had to watch your buying.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Like how?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, just not get stuck with anything, that's what I was, you know, what people wanted.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Now there's just certain things, they come up here from Lewiston and there's things I was special in, we worked shoes and gloves, see?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I had to know what they wanted, those were the boys from the mill. And if you carried it and you had your prize and didn't market, and most of these business firms, even Penny and all those guys, it's a one -way street, you know, and their markup isn't flexible, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And that's why, if you find out what their markup is, how good it is, how you can beat them, see?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Find out what they're making a profit on, just you stick with me to death, even Penny, find out what they're making a profit on, all them change you. Just get under them. Give them some of their own medicine.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you usually, in the early days, did you usually buy most of your things locally or was it mostly all brought in from other parts of the country?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, you had to bring in your, whatever, your wholesale house would have to be appropriate, your groceries were all your nearest points, you know, like Lewiston. Of course, there's a lot of Spokane, too. Of course, Spokane would meet, you know, give you your freight rebate or something, they'd be a competitive proposition.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But you bought your cattle and things locally?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh, yeah, that's all I mentioned.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Then you shipped them out?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: They went out, sure, we bought cattle.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Of course, we've been out of that business for years. Well, people have been out there, too. This isn't a cattle country like it used to be. Right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how long was this cattle country? What? How long was this cattle country? What do you mean, how? Well, until when?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Until it was more cattle raised around you. Still, cattle, big range back there in the mountains, still lots of cattle, but not like those in those days. They used to ship out of here, see, they don't do that anymore, it's all trucked out to Lewiston. Lewiston's are marketing spook instead of Spokane. That's about the biggest change that's been made, they truck to Lewiston instead of shipping freight to Spokane, which is much easier, see, they don't do that anymore.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: How many depressions did you survive? How many?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Two, three, two, I guess.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was it like here in your store during the big depressions, 28, 29?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: It was a barter deal, he couldn't barter, he couldn't trade while he was out of the picture, he just couldn't make it.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: There wasn't any money, see, and you just had to, can you?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Of course, we had to have a certain amount of money, but I'm sure it was a trade deal.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of trades were there?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, we, sure, we'd trade for anything that we could sell, anything they'd have. Of course, most of the livestock in our business, we'd trade for beans or wheat or peas or whatever, there wasn't no peas raised then, it was beans, beans and grain, hay, anything. Anything you could turn, anything is saleable. Right, right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But then you'd trade that on again to somebody else who wanted it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Or used it ourselves, so we asked if we could do it, so we'd do a trade, but if we had a feedlot out there and we had to cattle every morning, and even one time Dad used to feed hogs, after he got out of it, my little brother didn't want to have none to do it no more. I never was the outside man, I was always the inside man, I remember.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: When you used to ship your excess lard and chickens and things to Spokane and to Pullman?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Pullman was the country.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Both Pullman and Shipman. Whichever way they wanted, we'd ship live and dress.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But you kept going through the Depression just by trading?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was Kendrick a pretty quiet little town?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: It's been a staple town, you don't have these ups and downs, you know, you've got a certain amount of people that you deal with that pays their bills and says, she's all right here. There's lots of business we can get. We have good hardware, we have a good general store, like I had, but they had a good grocery, two groceries, and they have good dry goods, for instance. I had an old fashioned general store that had dry goods, meats, groceries, everything.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah. No, I didn't carry the ready to wear.

words: The ready to wear. Dressed stuff. I didn't go into men's clothes and stuff. Or suits. I stayed out of ladies stuff.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: No, no.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: That's the business that's old. Yeah. We've got a good hardware store in town here, though. There's always been a good hardware store here. As far as it's a good country, it's far, far away.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Right? Little farmers?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah.

words: Well, how about like, the social life here? I was talking about the quiet town, and I was wondering was there much to do?

words: Oh, sure. They still do. Yeah, they have parades and bands. They will do. That hasn't changed. We have ours on Locust Day and today instead of... That's our Fourth of July though. It's better than having them on the floor. Of course, now they go picnicking for people to go to celebrations.

words: There isn't much of a rodeo town. There are quite a few, though. Riding clubs have areas. I mean, it isn't exactly a rodeo.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: In the early days.

words: Well, I never had to keep one in my garage, you know.

words: Well, we opened at eight and closed at six. But, I'd say some of them used to stay overnight, you know, six days a week.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm. An hour a day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: An hour a day for a ten hour day?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I don't know. From eight to six. Mm -hmm. Nine. That's nine. That's ten.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: That's ten hours.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you clerking first? The first store you clerked in, was that also a mercantile store? Was that a different, a merchandise store?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: It was. With me, you see, they farmed me out. There was four of us brothers. Mm -hmm. And we was too many brothers. Well, I had two brothers. I had one brother. He was, I guess, tall as him. One brother had, he never would work in sight. And he had what we call in them days a drain line. Mm -hmm. And he run, he hauled all the stuff from the depot, you know, and did all the haulin' and all that stuff. And in fact, he made the store deliveries. And then my other brother had worked in the shop. I wasn't in the business then. I was out working in the dry goods store. And one of them run the shop and the other one, his dad, bought cattle and done the butcher. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And you worked in the dry goods store?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I worked in the giant store. Mm -hmm. And I come back here. 20, I come back in, in Belong and such. We bought out of the dry goods store. Mm -hmm. And I come back to run it. I come back and so. We got over here to meet Margaret and I kept the groceries. And I kept the dry goods. It was first. And I got Margaret five years ago.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was the last time, wasn't it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm. That was the end. Tell me about the dry goods. That first store, how big it was. Yardage, ready to wear for women. Men's tailored suits that were ordered and all that was done right in that store. Yeah, that's what I was told. I told them that. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Millinery, they made their own hats.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did, what was the price of things in those days? Like what was the price of a tailored suit, say?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, if you went in with a tailored suit, if you're a plutocrat, we'd want to sell you something up around $50, $60.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm. If you're just working out your wages and stuff, you'd stick pretty close to a $25 suit. Mm -hmm. You'd get them inside a suit for $20, $25. Oh yeah, $25, $25, yeah, you could buy it. There's more than any price you want to pay. Generally, the guys that want the good suit, the guys that want the expensive suits, they generally come in and want you to order them up and down. So it's measurement. Mm -hmm. Guaranteeing. Mm -hmm. Clothing was really, it was really pretty high them days. They were, well, it was high for them times. Mm -hmm. You know, pretty much in line the way it is now. Mm -hmm. Well, it wasn't as high, according to the shoes and stuff. It was high. Well, I told you, like me, that's, here's where the shoe business went to. Right. 50 % of them, that's rubber -soled, they'd, something like that. I had so much trouble with my feet and I couldn't get anything that fit me I could wear. I got broken arches, so I got to wearing these, and by the time I got them, I didn't know I had any feet, so I just kept on wearing them. Right. Families, you know, in the fall, families would come in and buy all their winter clothes. You know that, of course. One trip, they bought all their clothes for the whole family. They'd bring the kids in. Mm -hmm. And these boys got family and they grew up. I'd see them and see a kid. Oh, he was close. I don't know whether you'd call him Stingy, but he was tight. Of course, he had a, he had a large family. He had five or six boys, and they were just, it was there, after teenagers. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: And, he'd come in and he'd, I think it was five or six boys, wasn't it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh. They're all grown now, and these kids had got family. Anyway, he brought these sticks in. Mm -hmm. Each stick, man and boy, the size of his shoe that he could wear. Mm -hmm. Well, he'd make, get these sticks, you know, long enough so that he never brought any short ones in, too short, never come back. And he'd work that stick in there and pick out his shoes and he'd just ride one, so. Huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why didn't he bring the kids in?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: He just didn't have these clothes to come in and it just, he was that kind of a guy. Mm -hmm. He was paying for them shoes and he was buying them. Mm -hmm. Didn't have any chance. No argument, that way. You'd have to know him to know that. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And they all turned out good, didn't they, them guys?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, turned out good.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why didn't they?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Clem and Hailey are in business here in Kindred.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yes, sir, you can never forget. I think during the Depression there, we were selling shoes and come in and fitted them out and I think it was, believe this or not, his shoes were down to $1 .98 a pair. Mm -hmm. These kids' shoes. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And that was mostly in barter, too, wasn't it? That was mostly in barter, too, wasn't it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, he was barter in level, but he couldn't barter too much on that deal because he was a good price, he couldn't reach it anywhere. Mm -hmm. I didn't make too much on them, but I have some of them. I sold them for $1 .98 a pair. Mm -hmm. So you know they had to be off the cheap. Right. Nail shoes, sold as nails. Mm -hmm. They had to sell them. Now, either recall split ladder, or full -grained ladder. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: It's kind of hard to hear those parts revolving like. That's okay.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Does that go on there?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh yeah, probably. There's no big deal.

words: What other kind of characters were around? Any people you remember in particular had any odd, you know, experiences with them?

words: Well, the characters that come around those days, we call them Bachar bums. Mm -hmm. There were a bunch of characters. Mm -hmm. And they wear a boxcar. They sleep on, come in on these empty boxcars, you know. Mm -hmm. And they come in here and they sleep in the jungle. We had jungles around here. Mm -hmm. We call them jungles, hobo jungles. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that around the Depression times?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah. Mm -hmm. We were back there when we first came to Canada and jumped through the Depression. The Depression done, I guess that's what done away with it. Mm -hmm. There's never been any sense. They got stricter rules on the railroads, I guess. Well, they got, no, there's Social Security done that. Mm -hmm. Got work for people. Right, right. See, everybody gets certain aids. They get a medal anyway, you know. They get something. That's Social Security's what done away with that. Mm -hmm.

words: Uh -huh. You'd give them a little of anything that you had. Stale around or something you wanted to drink. Sometimes, scraps, you know. They'd take anything. Mm -hmm. They'd make up a stew, you know, hobo jungle. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. No, sir, we haven't, we haven't had any boxcar. There's never been any. That's what data did that when that went out was Social Security. Mm -hmm. And that is about when that started.

words: Put money in circulation. Mm -hmm. Name anything else. Nothing.

words: Yeah. That's ever put money in circulation like that has. Mm -hmm. An interesting pioneer story is your father, his father was in the freight in out here in deals with Lake Tahoe County.

words: At that time, before the railroad was built up to the, you know where we call Arrow Junction down here, where the fort says that you're grooved up to Orofino and then the railroad and then comes to Kendrick?

words: Mm -hmm. Well, that was the journey. That as far as the railroad, there was no railroad any farther than that. Mm -hmm. There was none up the river. Mm -hmm. See, Orofino had no railroad, or that, I mean that prairie. Mm -hmm. All that prairie country had no railroad of any kind. Mm -hmm. And this was the closest point, Kendrick. Mm -hmm. You see, it was so canny. If they went across here, if they went down, they'd pay more for it, you know, and then they'd have more grown up. Mm -hmm. But this was the shipping point. Mm -hmm. There are certain beans that go through that wasn't edible, but you couldn't get them out of the cleaner. Mm -hmm. And so, you'd have to hand pick them. Mm -hmm. Gee, people here, I remember these women, these widow women especially, picked beans all the way up to the coast. And they'd pick beans all winter. Mm -hmm. And pick those beans. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Well, I guess they still do it, Chet, don't they? Raise beans and peas for seed, don't they?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I think so.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Hand pick that stuff?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm. I think they still do it. Mm -hmm. They haven't come over any better yet.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Get certified seed, I still think they have to do it, don't they?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm. I don't know, I haven't been around, but I don't think they have.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, after you've gone out and made this money, what did you usually spend it on?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, I'll tell you, we wasn't getting very big money. Mm -hmm. Dad was pretty good to us. He wouldn't make us, unless we was working on some good job or something, he wouldn't make us. I paid one little one. Mm -hmm. You know that when I was working studying in the store, and getting paid for the month. That's when I was sick and buying that junkie temperature, I think. Mm -hmm. By the time you've bought your clothes and paid board, if you was working out, you couldn't have too much to worry about. Yeah. And we, of course we had to have a weekend, but every week or two, there was dances. Mm -hmm. Nice to go to.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they have fiddlers come down and play the dances, or local people?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: They had orchids.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: They had rag arts, good music. We had a lot of good music here in town. I don't know, it was pretty fortunate.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Mostly local people?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh, yeah. See, we had a homeless family, we used to live right over there and they were gone musicians. Well, this Walt Thomas, he went and spoke to the band leader up there and was doing the army.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: He wanted to enlist in the army in the band. The army said, well, they couldn't take any enlistments. Of course, they hadn't got any leaders, they didn't have any band leaders. They didn't take any leachates. Well, he says, I'm your man. He says, I'm a band leader. He took his examination down. He left this band all the time, all the ones he went overseas with.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I don't know, that was World War I, wasn't it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah. Yeah, he went overseas with his band.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They used to play, what kind of music would they play for dances? What? What kind of music would they play?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh, mostly violin, piano.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Square dance type tunes or polkas or what?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, not much square dance in those days, Roger. Mostly, when I danced with them, my dance was mostly waltzes, waltzes and two steps. And by theirself, they'd have a cadre or whatever it was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did they call that, quadrilles? What was it?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: I think so....

speaker: Marsha Long
words: I wasn't there....

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: No.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Didn't have any more school systems in those days. No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: This was probably quite a bit quieter back then, too, wasn't it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh, that's right.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: There's much streets, there's much streets. Horses.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: They have more sidewalks.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The sidewalk was that high?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, come right off the edge or... By the street.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I guess it was a little bit quieter then, too, wasn't it?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That's quieter?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah. Oh, yeah. They had a shivaree or something. There were a bunch of drunks sometimes. Yeah.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, pretty quiet. Horses don't do this.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, I've seen the first cars, motor cars, come in this country.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where do they come from?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, they ship them. Oh, four -wheelers.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind? You've seen the picture?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Oh, sure. Well, I think the one we, the dealer here, I think it was International. I think they were.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: A dealer came into town to start selling cars?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: No, there was a dealer here. These hardwares, the ones that sold them.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Didn't they?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: See that?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: There was a four -wheeler and a one -seated buggy tire. And one -seated.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Did you ever see one of the old ones?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Yeah, I think I have.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: What were people's reactions to the first cars that came in here? What did they think of them?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: They were excited.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: You know, and you'd get killed going up and down these grays, you know, and all that stuff. Yeah. See, once they got killed, first thing they knew everybody was dying. Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the first ones come in by train or did they drive in by themselves or what?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I don't remember. Well, they were shipped. I imagine the distributor of Moscow, I imagine. No, they didn't get so many out in the first thing. People didn't buy them. They'd buy them now.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: See that?

speaker: Marvin Long
words: A fella got a car to run a, well, he generally had some kind of a commercial car. He wouldn't be buying one. You know, just to go out and buy a car. Most of them were commercial cars.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You mean cars that people run their businesses with or what?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, yeah, for delivering, you know, and stuff.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: The one they started to come, they come pretty fast. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About what year was that? Oh, no, what year would that have been?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Let's see, we come down here in 1908. The very first one around that time, I think.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: See, World War I was 18, wasn't it? 18 to 20?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm. Just before World War I. About in there is when it started. Mm -hmm. I think.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the wars have any effect on life out here besides, you know, having something else to read in the paper?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, I'll say. World War I, putting everybody had somebody over there fighting. Mm -hmm. Dad couldn't go out to work. He'd walk cattle here and stuff. He had a brother over there, front line trenches in World War I. He couldn't leave this town until the paper come in about 9 o 'clock to get the daily paper and read that casualty list. That's how much he was worried about. Just see whether Tom and Brother Tom had spared another day or not. It was awful. What they went through with, the grandparents.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mother seemed to take it a lot better than Dad did. Dad didn't take it. He couldn't take it. Well, it was an awful thing. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: You know, you'd have to have a son over there in the trenches in order to realize it, wouldn't it?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm. Some of them. Some of them was filling their pockets and that was good to see. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that even happening out here? What? The filling your pockets?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Sure. Sure. There was everywhere.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: What kind of businesses out here could profit by...

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, there's some of them. Raising prices. Wheat. Wheat went up. They was praying for wheat to go up. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: The more they killed over there, the more wheat it took, see?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Isn't that something?

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Yeah. That's very... One guy was mourning over his son being over there in the trenches and the other one was glorifying him because he was keeping his sweet price up. Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did that same thing happen in the Second War?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Not as bad, no.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: It didn't last very long.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, it lasted about six years.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: War II?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah. The war wasn't as rough either. The memorial to the boys of the First World War was the flagpole in the cart down here. Mm -hmm. And they moved it just this last year and the big metal ball on top contained a list. Huh. Yeah. They didn't replace that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did they do with that ball?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: They moved it over by the Second War Memorial there on the corner. The swimming pool at the memorial for the Second World War. There's a big box with all the names of the boys on the corner there. And this first one, a copy of the Gazette with all their names was in that ball on the top of the flagpole. They gave the ball to get over here pretty easy. Huh. I think.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Where did they put the ball back?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I don't think they put it back. I think he's got the paper in the way that was in there.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: See, who's that?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: That's one. Okay. That takes a lot of effort. Oh, it takes a lot of effort. You've got it out there. Oh, it's okay. There's a girl that lives in here going, you know, she's a fool or something. She don't have any set though.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What have we covered at this point? What other stories did you tell me before we started the machine run?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: You haven't told me. I'm trying to recall now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You haven't said much about the time you spent when you were homesteading up, was it around Leland that you were homesteading?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Dad homesteaded when he come out here in 88. He come out and homesteaded up here on Cedar Creek. Took out a timber. They called it them days, a stone timber claim. The homesteads. He had the rights to do that.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: And, let's see.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: And he made his living by making shakes and peddling them out on the farmers on Potlatch Ridge.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long was he out there?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, he was up in Windex Hill. He come in 88 and one day he was up in this place. In 88.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: You were real small.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, let's see. He was born in 94.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did he give up the homestead?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Because of the bettering himself. He come out and rented a farm on Potlatch Ridge.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was the apple farm?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: No, that was a grain farm. And he got, it seemed so bad that he had to quit farming.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: And we went to Leland. We got meat business.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Come there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you prove up a rock and timber claim the same way you proved up a regular homestead?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, if you wanted to take it. You could develop it if you had the right. At that time when he come in here, if he got out here on the ridge, he could have got some of this farmland. And you just waited until somebody throwed it up. You could get a homestead and a stone and timber and a preemption.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: You had three rights when they first come in here. They were that eager to get settlers.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was the preemption?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, it was just a, I don't know exactly what it is, but we took up some of the best land we was taking up out of the farm. It was a preemption. I just never quite understood what that is. It wasn't the old time, they should have told me, I guess. But anyway, that's what we could have. And some of them got some of this stone and timber. They just had a few stones on it and a few trees. They called it stone and timber and got by with it. They got pretty strict on that. But this preemption was, I don't understand it, I know of one outfit up there on the Potlatch Ridge, one of these huffins. They were really big there, these grandparents. They took up preemption in homesteads. And they got control of a lot of land up there. And I guess the Stellas and some of them were families yet. But that's what they had. That's how eager they were to get people to develop this country. Of course, I think that stone and timber was abused by privilege.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were you supposed to do with the stone and timber?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: You had certain improvements you had to make on it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What's the difference between stone and timber in a regular homestead?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: A regular homestead was anything. This farmland up here was all come under homesteads. But on the edges up there, they'd take fringes. That's where the fringes come in. The stone and timber is where you developed the stone or timber. Oh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Like you were supposed to quarry stone or do some logging or something?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: If it was that kind of a stone, we didn't have any right in here. But there is sections that do have a church.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, around the mining countries, there's a lot of that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Could you get more land on the stone and timber thing or what?

speaker: Marvin Long
words: The homestead gave you 160 acres. All right. Your preemption gave you 160 acres. And then your regular homestead.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: I gave you stone and timber, didn't I?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Then your regular homestead gave you three quarters if you were in a position to accept it. And of course, some of them were.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: You had to develop different things on each section.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: I think then if you pull some strings or something too, it had something to do with it. I noticed most of them had that all and was pretty badly criticized by the old -timers. I don't just understand it too much.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where were the people? Your dad just had the stone and timber though, right? Or did he have more than that?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Well, no. I think he took up, I think, under just the homestead. I don't think he ever took any stone and timber. But he could. That's the opportunity. I guess what I was telling you when this came in, that he had an opportunity to get a stone and timber and didn't take it. And you'd always regretted it. You didn't use up that right. Because it wasn't long afterwards that you couldn't do it anymore. But he could have had those.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: You see what I mean?

speaker: Marvin Long
words: And people did take it. They'd prove up, live so long on this, and then come out and get a stone and timber. And do the same thing on it. Right. Yeah, see three rights.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: What did I say?

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Stone and timber and homestead.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Preemption. Preemption, yeah.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Then you lived in Leland and your mother ran a hotel.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: And what'd your dad do there?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: That's when you had the meat market. We run the cattle and the meat market. We peddle meat. We cut it up. We put up ice in the wintertime. And these meat wagons would build the boxes on behind these hags. And then it was placed down in the bottom for ice. And we'd ice them up in the morning. And take out this, cut this meat up and peddle it all over the potlight ridge. And I was just big enough to go around looking for gates. Oh, I cut them in the meantime. We peddled it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would you take one row today or something? Would you have a regular route?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: We had routes.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: We had routes. We'd generally make six days a week. We'd generally have three routes and make them every other.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: See how that'd be?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: We'd make them every other day.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: But you could have made them pretty often. Didn't have any way of keeping them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would you keep your ice?

speaker: Marvin Long
words: We had ice houses.

speaker: Marvin Long
words: Did you ever see an ice house? Did you ever hear of one?

speaker: Marvin Long
words: I've heard of them. I've never seen one. Well, it's just a building, shed, any kind of a building. Of course, the better answer is the better. We used to have our ice houses right over across the alley to right street, across here. And a sheet iron building. And you just build up your ice in the center and you pack that good with sawdust all around it. And the sawdust is what kept it. You had to watch it all the way to see that your ice didn't get in the air. You had to have air connections. But we'd get pretty well through the season with it. We'd have quite a shred to take them in. After you opened it up, you had more shankage. But the capital sawdust. We didn't have no refrigeration.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Then you'd pack the ice in the meat wagon and pack meat boxes on top of that?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: We'd have the meat on trays and stuff and have this ice underneath. And this meat was all cut and put on trays. And they'd come out to get by and we had scales there. And they'd wait it on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: By the pound rather than by the cut?

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Yeah, by the pound. Took me along to open gates.

speaker: Marvin Long or Marsha Long
words: Every farmer...
